2.3.a.info:
Cisco Discovery Protocol (CDP) is Cisco's proprietary Layer 2 discovery protocol. Its single job is to help Cisco devices learn about each other without needing any IP configuration — hostname, model number, IOS version, interface name, and management IP are all exchanged at Layer 2 the moment two Cisco devices are cabled together. CDP has been on every Cisco product — routers, switches, APs, firewalls, IP phones — since the mid-1990s, and it remains on by default to this day.

How it operates: CDP generates raw Ethernet frames (with SNAP header) and sends them to the multicast MAC 01:00:0C:CC:CC:CC. That multicast address is recognized only by Cisco devices; a non-Cisco switch sitting in the path treats it as an ordinary L2 multicast and floods it locally, which is fine because CDP is strictly one-hop. The frames are never wrapped in IP, so a brand-new router with no IP address configured can still announce itself and see its neighbors.

Why it exists: Before CDP, if a tech unplugged a cable in a closet they had no fast way to know which device was on the other end. CDP lets you log into one switch and instantly see every directly-connected Cisco device — hostname, port, model — eliminating the need to physically trace cables. This is the #1 troubleshooting tool in the Cisco world and the reason the exam treats CDP as mandatory knowledge.

Boundaries: CDP is Cisco-to-Cisco only. Any non-Cisco neighbor (Arista, Juniper, HPE, Dell) will not appear in CDP output — for those you need LLDP. CDP is also unidirectional: each device advertises its own information, but does not query neighbors. You learn about a neighbor only if that neighbor is sending CDP frames back.

Exam framing: The two facts the exam tests repeatedly are (1) CDP is Cisco proprietary, and (2) CDP is enabled by default. Mix those up with LLDP (IEEE, disabled by default) and you will miss a guaranteed question.

hack.memory: CDP = 'Cisco Devices Phoning home.' Three anchors: (1) Cisco-proprietary. (2) Layer 2 — no IP needed. (3) ON by default. Multicast MAC is a Cisco org-unique address — 01:00:0C:CC:CC:CC. If the question says 'vendor-neutral' or 'IEEE standard,' it's LLDP, not CDP.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, drop two Cisco routers with a crossover cable. Do NOT configure any IP addresses. Run show cdp neighbors on each router after ~60 seconds — both see each other. This single lab proves CDP operates without IP. Then swap one router for a generic switch — the neighbor disappears because CDP doesn't cross non-Cisco devices.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 opens with this exact concept. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11 gives the L2 multicast address and historical context. Exam: 'Which protocol is Cisco-proprietary and operates at Layer 2?' → CDP.

2.3.a.info:
CDP is enabled by default on every modern Cisco IOS, IOS-XE, and NX-OS device the moment it is powered on — no configuration required. This is one of the most exam-relevant facts in all of Domain 2, because its counterpart (LLDP) is disabled by default on the same hardware.

Why Cisco ships it on: CDP is a network-management convenience. Cisco wants techs to be able to plug two boxes together, log in, and immediately see the topology. The product is more useful out of the box when CDP 'just works.' The tradeoff — information leakage — is accepted as a default because most Cisco gear lives inside trusted networks.

How to confirm state: show cdp displays the global CDP configuration. If you see 'CDP is not enabled', someone has run no cdp run. If CDP is running you will see the global timer (60s), holdtime (180s), and advertisement version (v2). On a per-interface basis, show cdp interface lists every port CDP is actively sending from.

Policy implication: Because CDP is on by default, every newly deployed Cisco device starts broadcasting hostname, management IP, IOS version, and model to every directly-connected neighbor. On an internet-facing or DMZ interface, that is a reconnaissance gift to an attacker. Security hardening guides (Cisco's own and CIS benchmarks) require no cdp enable on any interface that leaves the trust boundary — and in the highest-security zones, no cdp run globally.

Exam twist: Candidates memorize 'CDP is on' and 'LLDP is off' but still get tripped up by wording like 'which protocol requires the run command to enable?' — the answer is LLDP (because CDP does not require the command; it's already running). Read the question carefully.

hack.memory: CDP = 'Comes Defaulted Powered-on.' LLDP = 'Lazy, Leaves Device Powered-off.' Opposite defaults is the #1 CDP vs LLDP exam question. If you only memorize one fact from this topic, memorize this one.
hack.practice: Load a fresh Cisco router in Packet Tracer. Without touching config, run show cdp — it confirms CDP is running. Now run show lldp — it reports LLDP is not enabled. Flip each: no cdp run and lldp run. Reverse the defaults and verify with the same show commands. This 2-minute drill burns the default states permanently.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 hammers this opposite-default fact repeatedly. OCG Chapter 11 lists defaults in a table. Exam: expect 1-2 questions that hinge on which protocol is on/off by default. Memorize: CDP=ON, LLDP=OFF.

2.3.a.info:
CDP uses two timers: the advertisement timer (how often CDP frames are sent) and the holdtime (how long a neighbor entry remains valid if no refresh arrives). The defaults are 60 seconds and 180 seconds respectively — a 3:1 ratio that gives two chances to receive a refresh before a neighbor is aged out.

Why 3x the advertisement interval? If the holdtime equaled the advertisement interval, a single dropped CDP frame would cause a neighbor to falsely expire. A 3x multiplier means you can lose two consecutive advertisements (e.g., 120 seconds of silence) before the entry disappears — a safety cushion against transient loss. This '3x the hello' pattern shows up again in OSPF (dead = 4x hello) and HSRP (hold = 3x hello).

Tuning: Both timers are globally configurable: cdp timer [seconds] (5–254) and cdp holdtime [seconds] (10–255). Lowering the timer (e.g., 10 seconds) speeds up neighbor detection when a link flaps but increases CPU and bandwidth overhead — relevant in dense topologies. Raising it reduces overhead but delays failure detection. Most production networks leave the defaults.

The Holdtme column in show cdp neighbors: This column displays the remaining seconds before a neighbor ages out, not the configured holdtime. Each received CDP advertisement resets the counter to 180. If you watch show cdp neighbors twice in a row, the Holdtme value will have decremented — proof that CDP entries are time-bounded, not permanent.

Exam framing: 'What is the default CDP advertisement interval?' → 60 seconds. 'What is the default CDP holdtime?' → 180 seconds. 'What is the ratio?' → 3:1. These numbers are near-certain on the exam; pair them with LLDP's 30s/120s (4:1) in a flashcard.

states: t=180: neighbor expires if no frame

hack.memory: CDP = 60/180 = 'One minute on, three strikes you're out.' Every 60s a CDP ad goes out. Holdtime = 3x the send timer. Miss two in a row and the neighbor disappears. LLDP is faster: 30/120. Both use the 'holdtime = 4x' style pattern (well, 3x for CDP, 4x for LLDP — memorize both pairs).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, form a CDP adjacency between two switches. Run show cdp neighbors — note the Holdtme value (e.g., 172). Wait 10 seconds, run it again — it should now read ~162. Pull the cable; keep polling. Around the 180-second mark the entry disappears. You've just watched the holdtime expire in real time.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 quizzes on the timers. OCG Chapter 11 includes a timer table. Exam guaranteed: at least one question testing 60/180 (CDP) vs 30/120 (LLDP). Write these four numbers on your exam whiteboard FIRST.

2.3.a.info:
A CDP advertisement is a structured package of facts the sending device knows about itself. The core fields carried in every CDPv2 advertisement are: Device ID (hostname), Address (management IP), Platform (hardware model string), Capabilities (R=Router, S=Switch, T=Trans Bridge, B=Source Route Bridge, H=Host, I=IGMP, r=Repeater), Port ID (the local interface sending the frame), Version (IOS/NX-OS version), Duplex, Native VLAN, and VTP Management Domain.

Why these specific fields? CDP was designed to answer the questions a tech asks when they are in front of a switch staring at a blinking port: 'What is this connected to? Where? Running what software? Which VLAN?' Every field maps to one of those questions. The inclusion of VTP domain and native VLAN is a tell that CDP assumes a Cisco-centric LAN where those concepts matter.

Basic vs detail — what's gated: show cdp neighbors shows Device ID, Local Intrfce, Holdtme, Capability, Platform, and Port ID — a terse 6-column summary. The Address, Version, Native VLAN, VTP domain, and Duplex fields only appear in show cdp neighbors detail. This is the single most-tested distinction in Domain 2.3: if the exam asks 'how do you find a neighbor's IP address?' the answer is always show cdp neighbors detail.

Capability codes decoded: Most Cisco devices advertise multiple capabilities at once — for example, a Catalyst 9300 typically advertises 'R S I' (Router, Switch, IGMP) because it can route, switch, and participate in multicast. A pure L2 switch advertises 'S I'. An IP phone advertises 'H' (Host) or 'T' depending on vintage.

Security angle: Every one of these fields is a reconnaissance nugget. Platform + Version together point directly to a CVE database — an attacker who sees 'Cisco 2960, IOS 12.2(55)SE1' knows exactly which privilege-escalation bugs apply. This is why external-facing ports should run no cdp enable.

layers: TLVs: Device ID, Address, Port ID, Capabilities, Version, Platform, VTP, Native VLAN, Duplex

hack.memory: CDP payload mnemonic: 'Daddy Always Plays Catch, Producing Violent Near-miss Dunks' = Device ID, Address, Platform, Capabilities, Port ID, Version, Native VLAN, Duplex. Or simpler: remember the basic 6 (Device ID, Local Intrfce, Holdtme, Capability, Platform, Port ID) and the detail 4 (Address/IP, Version, Native VLAN, VTP domain, Duplex).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, build a 3-device chain. Run show cdp neighbors — write down every column. Now run show cdp neighbors detail — list every NEW field that appears. The deltas are your flashcard. Verify the IP address only shows up in detail. Do this drill twice on different topologies.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 walks through a detail output line by line. OCG Chapter 11. Exam pattern: 'You run show cdp neighbors. You need the IP of your neighbor — what command?' → show cdp neighbors detail. Memorize which fields are basic vs detail.

2.3.a.info:
CDP is strictly one-hop — it never traverses more than a single Layer 2 segment. A Cisco switch will not relay a received CDP frame out any other port. If you are on Switch A and a Cisco router is two switches away (A → B → R), you will NOT see the router in A's CDP table. You will only see Switch B.

Why one-hop: CDP frames are sent to the multicast MAC 01:00:0C:CC:CC:CC, which is in the IEEE 802.1D reserved range (01:80:C2:00:00:00 – 01:80:C2:00:00:0F uses its own rules, but the CDP MAC behaves similarly). Cisco switches consume these frames at the ingress port — they do not forward them. The protocol was designed to discover only directly-connected neighbors; if it forwarded, you would see every Cisco device in the entire broadcast domain, which defeats the point of mapping cable adjacency.

Unidirectional behavior: CDP is also unidirectional — every device announces itself, but it does not query. A router says 'I am R1, here is my info' on every port. Its neighbors listen and record. There is no request/response handshake. This makes CDP lightweight (no state to track) but also means a silent neighbor is invisible — if you disable CDP on one end, that device vanishes from the neighbor's table after the holdtime expires.

Mapping a topology with CDP: Because it's one-hop, mapping a multi-device network requires hopping: SSH into Switch A → show cdp neighbors → see Switch B → SSH into Switch B → show cdp neighbors → see Switch C → repeat. Cisco Prime Infrastructure and DNA Center automate this traversal to build full topology maps.

Exam framing: 'CDP can only discover…' → directly-connected neighbors. 'Is CDP transitive?' → NO. 'Will CDP cross a router?' → NO (and it wouldn't anyway because routers consume CDP frames). These are fast recall questions.

nodes: Switch B (2 hops — INVISIBLE to R1)

hack.memory: CDP = 'Can Discover only Peers directly attached.' One hop, one wire, done. Think cabled neighbor only — never two houses down the street. If the exam asks whether CDP can see a device through an intermediate switch, the answer is NO.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, chain three switches: SW1 ↔ SW2 ↔ SW3. On SW1, run show cdp neighbors — you see only SW2. SSH into SW2 — you see SW1 and SW3. SSH into SW3 — you see only SW2. This chain-traversal pattern is how real topology discovery works with CDP.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam-relevant phrasing: 'directly connected,' 'not transitive,' 'one hop.' Also know: CDP is unidirectional — each device announces, no querying.

2.3.a.info:
no cdp enable is an interface-level command that disables CDP on a single port without touching the rest of the device. This is the primary security hardening technique for CDP — you keep it globally enabled (so internal ports can still discover neighbors) and selectively disable it on external-facing interfaces.

When to disable: Any interface that leaves the trust boundary of your network should have CDP off. Typical candidates: internet-facing router interfaces (WAN ports to ISPs), DMZ firewall ports, guest/lobby switchports, camera-network ports, IoT segments, kiosk ports, vendor/contractor ports. The rule of thumb: if an untrusted device could be on the other end, disable CDP.

What attackers see if you don't: A laptop plugged into a CDP-enabled port will passively receive a CDP frame every 60 seconds containing the switch's hostname, management IP, IOS version, hardware platform, native VLAN, VTP domain, and the port number. Tools like Wireshark and yersinia parse these frames automatically. The attacker can now pivot: target the management IP with vendor-specific exploits matched to the exact IOS version, VLAN-hop into the native VLAN, etc. This is not theoretical — it's a standard penetration-testing first step.

Configuration:

Switch(config)# interface Gi0/1
Switch(config-if)# no cdp enable

Verify with show cdp interface Gi0/1 — the interface should not appear, or should show 'CDP is not enabled'. Re-enable with cdp enable.

Global vs per-interface: no cdp run kills CDP everywhere; no cdp enable kills it on one port. Per-interface is almost always the right choice — you still want CDP running internally for troubleshooting.

Exam framing: 'Which command disables CDP on a specific port?' → no cdp enable (not no cdp run — that's global). 'When should CDP be disabled?' → on untrusted or external-facing interfaces.

hack.memory: Security rule: 'internal = on, external = off.' Per-port disable is no cdp enable (interface config). Global disable is no cdp run (global config). Don't confuse them — the exam loves this distinction.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: on a switch with two ports, show cdp interface — both visible. Configure interface Gi0/1 → no cdp enable. Re-run show cdp interface — Gi0/1 disappeared. The neighbor on Gi0/1 will lose its CDP entry after 180 seconds (holdtime). Re-enable with cdp enable.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Cisco's own hardening guide (and CIS benchmarks) mandate no cdp enable on external interfaces. Exam: 1 question on the interface-level disable command and its security rationale.

2.3.a.info:
The show cdp neighbors family is the most-used CDP command set in real operations and on the exam. There are two flavors — basic and detail — and the exam tests the difference relentlessly.

show cdp neighbors (basic): Six columns, one row per neighbor. Device ID (neighbor's hostname), Local Intrfce (your port connected to this neighbor), Holdtme (seconds left on the entry), Capability (R/S/T/B/H/I/r codes), Platform (hardware model like WS-C2960-24TT-L), Port ID (the neighbor's port connected to you). Notably absent: management IP, IOS version, native VLAN, VTP domain, duplex.

show cdp neighbors detail: One full paragraph per neighbor instead of a row. Adds the missing fields: Management IP address, IOS/NX-OS version string, Native VLAN, VTP Domain, Duplex, CDP advertisement version. The IP address is the single most valuable addition — it's how you find a device you can see at Layer 2 but need to reach at Layer 3 to SSH into.

Reading a detail block:

Device ID: SW2.example.com
Entry address(es):
  IP address: 10.1.1.2
Platform: cisco WS-C2960-24TT-L, Capabilities: Switch IGMP
Interface: GigabitEthernet0/1, Port ID (outgoing port): GigabitEthernet0/24
Holdtime : 142 sec
Version :
  Cisco IOS Software, C2960 Software (C2960-LANBASEK9-M), Version 15.0(2)SE11, RELEASE SOFTWARE (fc3)
advertisement version: 2
Native VLAN: 1
Duplex: full
Management address(es):
  IP address: 10.1.1.2

Related show commands: show cdp (global CDP timer and holdtime), show cdp interface (per-port CDP state), show cdp entry [device-id] (detail for one specific neighbor by name — useful in large tables), show cdp traffic (packet statistics).

Exam pattern: The exam shows you output and asks you to extract a field. If the field is an IP or version string, the output must be detail. If the question gives you basic output and asks for an IP, the correct answer is 'run show cdp neighbors detail.'

hack.memory: Basic = 6 columns (no IP). Detail = full paragraph + IP/version/VLAN/VTP/duplex. Golden rule: IP is only in detail. Every exam question about 'find the IP of the neighbor' answers to show cdp neighbors detail.
hack.practice: Lab 3 devices in Packet Tracer. Run both commands and diff the outputs on paper. Highlight every field that only appears in detail — those are the exam's high-value fields. Do the same with show cdp entry [hostname] to see single-neighbor detail.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 walks through both command outputs. OCG Chapter 11 includes sample captures. Exam: 2–3 questions on parsing show cdp neighbors output — know what lives where.

2.3.b.info:
The six columns of show cdp neighbors are the skeleton of CDP output and the basis for most exam simlet questions. You need to read them fluently — not just recognize them, but know which column answers which question.

Column 1 — Device ID: The neighbor's configured hostname (from hostname global command). If no hostname is set, you see the default 'Switch' or 'Router,' which on a production network is a red flag. In multi-domain environments the hostname sometimes appears as an FQDN (SW2.example.com) because ip domain-name is configured.

Column 2 — Local Intrfce: YOUR interface that is receiving the neighbor's CDP frames. This is 'the port on my switch that is connected to this neighbor.' It is abbreviated (Gig 0/1 instead of GigabitEthernet0/1) to fit the table width. Use this column to trace cables.

Column 3 — Holdtme: Seconds remaining before this entry ages out. Starts at 180 (CDP default) and counts down. Each received advertisement resets it to 180. If you see a Holdtme near 10, the neighbor stopped sending — investigate.

Column 4 — Capability: One or more single-letter codes indicating what the neighbor is. Most devices show multiple letters (e.g., 'R S I' = Router, Switch, IGMP). The codes legend is printed above the table.

Column 5 — Platform: The hardware model string advertised by the neighbor. May be abbreviated (just 'cisco' on some platforms) or full (WS-C2960-24TT-L, ISR4321, N9K-C93180YC-EX). This tells you what you're dealing with at a glance.

Column 6 — Port ID: The neighbor's interface on the other end of the cable. This is the field you use to correlate 'my port Gi0/1 is cabled to neighbor's Gi0/24.' Together with Column 2 it fully describes the link.

Exam use: Simlets show a show cdp neighbors output and ask 'which port of the neighbor is connected to this switch?' → Port ID. 'What is the hostname?' → Device ID. 'What local port receives CDP from R2?' → Local Intrfce. Know the column meanings cold.

hack.memory: Six columns, two belong to YOU (Local Intrfce), four describe THEM (Device ID, Holdtme, Capability, Platform, Port ID — and Port ID is theirs). 'My port / Their port' is the key cable-tracing pair: Local Intrfce + Port ID.
hack.practice: Print a sample show cdp neighbors output. Quiz yourself: circle which columns describe YOU, which describe THEM. Then rebuild the topology on paper from the output alone — that's exactly what the exam simlet asks you to do.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 drills the columns. OCG Chapter 11 has sample outputs. Exam: you WILL read show cdp neighbors output under time pressure. Fluency matters.

2.3.b.info:
The Capability column of show cdp neighbors uses single-letter codes to describe what a neighbor IS. Most devices advertise multiple capabilities at once. Memorize all seven.

R — Router: Device performs Layer 3 routing. Any router, or a multilayer switch with ip routing enabled.

S — Switch: Device performs Layer 2 switching. Any standard Cisco switch (2960, 3560, 3650, 9300, etc.).

T — Trans Bridge: Transparent bridging device. Rare on modern CCNA networks; legacy terminology from early LAN-to-LAN interconnects.

B — Source Route Bridge: Token Ring source-route bridging. Obsolete but still in the code. You will essentially never see this in practice.

H — Host: End-station device (not a network device). Some Cisco IP phones and wireless access points identify as Host. Servers with Cisco NIC drivers sometimes advertise CDP as H.

I — IGMP: Device participates in IGMP (multicast group management). Nearly all Cisco switches advertise I alongside S because modern switches snoop IGMP by default.

r — Repeater: Legacy hub behavior. Essentially extinct; present for backward compatibility. Lowercase r to avoid confusion with R for Router.

Typical combos: A pure L2 switch = 'S I'. A multilayer switch with routing enabled = 'R S I'. A router = 'R' (sometimes 'R I' if multicast routing is on). An IP phone = 'H' or 'T H' (newer models). An AP = 'T' or 'B' depending on firmware.

Exam framing: The exam often shows output with 'R S I' and asks 'what is this device?' → a multilayer switch (it routes AND switches AND does multicast). 'What does the B code mean?' → Source Route Bridge. Memorize all seven — they come up both in CDP and (with different letters) in LLDP.

hack.memory: Mnemonic: 'Really Smart Techs Build Happy Internal routers' = R, S, T, B, H, I, (r). Lowercase r at the end stands out. Most-seen in practice: R, S, I. The other four are rare on modern networks but still on the exam.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, put a router, a switch, a multilayer switch (3560), and a PC in a topology with CDP enabled. Run show cdp neighbors from each device. Match each Capability string to the device type. Multilayer switch shows 'R S I' — routes, switches, IGMP-snoops all at once.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 lists all codes. OCG Chapter 11. Note LLDP has different codes (B=Bridge for switches, T=Telephone, W=WLAN AP) — don't mix them up.

2.3.b.info:
show cdp neighbors detail is the command that unlocks the fields hidden from the basic output. This is the single most-tested CDP command on the exam because it is the only one that reveals the neighbor's management IP address and IOS version.

What detail adds (vs basic):

  • Management IP address — the neighbor's configured IP (usually on a management SVI or loopback). Essential for knowing where to SSH.
  • IOS / NX-OS version string — full software version including release (e.g., '15.0(2)SE11'). Pairs with Platform to identify exact firmware and any applicable CVEs.
  • Native VLAN — the untagged VLAN on the trunk link (if the port is a trunk). Helps troubleshoot native-VLAN mismatches.
  • VTP Management Domain — the VTP domain name if VTP is configured. Useful when chasing VTP issues.
  • Duplex — the neighbor's duplex setting (full/half). Helps troubleshoot auto-negotiation mismatches.
  • CDP advertisement version — 1 or 2. Virtually always 2 on modern gear.

Practical workflow: You log into SW1 and need to SSH into the router cabled to SW1's Gi0/1. show cdp neighbors tells you which router is there but not its IP. Run show cdp neighbors detail, find the neighbor entry, copy the Management IP, ssh [ip] — done. This is a daily troubleshooting flow in production networks.

Security flip-side: The same information is valuable to an attacker. Platform + Version string is enough to find a public CVE. This is why CDP should be disabled on untrusted ports (see no cdp enable).

Output structure: Detail output is paragraph-style, one block per neighbor, separated by lines of dashes. Each block contains the full CDP TLV set the neighbor advertised. On a large network this output can be long — pipe through | section [hostname] or use show cdp entry [device-id] to target one neighbor.

Exam framing: 'How do you find the IP address of a directly connected neighbor?' → show cdp neighbors detail. 'Which command shows the IOS version of a neighbor?' → show cdp neighbors detail. If the question is about any field beyond the basic 6 columns, the answer is detail.

hack.memory: Detail = IP + Version + VLAN + VTP + Duplex. The IP address is the marquee field — if an exam question mentions 'IP of the neighbor' the answer is always show cdp neighbors detail. No exceptions.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, run both commands on the same switch, side by side. Circle every field in detail that is NOT in basic. You now have a single-page flashcard that maps the two commands.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 uses detail throughout. OCG Chapter 11 shows full sample output. Exam: 2–3 questions hinge on knowing detail = IP + version. Do not get tricked into picking show ip interface brief — that shows YOUR IPs, not your neighbor's.

2.3.b.info:
show cdp interface displays per-interface CDP status — which ports are actively sending CDP and what timers they are using. It is the fastest way to confirm CDP is running on a specific port and to troubleshoot 'why isn't my neighbor showing up?'

What it shows: For every interface where CDP is enabled, you see the interface name, encapsulation type (ARPA on Ethernet), the sending interval (default 60s), and the holdtime (default 180s). Interfaces where CDP is disabled (via no cdp enable) do NOT appear in the output at all — that's the giveaway.

Typical output:

GigabitEthernet0/1 is up, line protocol is up
  Encapsulation ARPA
  Sending CDP packets every 60 seconds
  Holdtime is 180 seconds
GigabitEthernet0/2 is up, line protocol is up
  Encapsulation ARPA
  Sending CDP packets every 60 seconds
  Holdtime is 180 seconds

If Gi0/3 is missing from this output and you expected a neighbor there, one of three things happened: (1) no cdp enable was run on that interface, (2) no cdp run was run globally, or (3) the interface is down/admin-shut. Confirm with show running-config interface Gi0/3 and show interfaces Gi0/3.

Narrowing to one port: show cdp interface Gi0/1 shows just that interface's CDP status. Useful on a 48-port switch where you don't want to scroll.

Relationship to other show commands: show cdp = global config. show cdp interface = per-port enablement and timers. show cdp neighbors = who is actually learned via CDP. Use them in that order when troubleshooting 'my neighbor isn't appearing.'

Exam framing: Less common than the neighbors commands but still testable. 'Which command shows whether CDP is sending on a specific interface?' → show cdp interface. 'If a neighbor is missing from show cdp neighbors, where do you check first?' → show cdp interface to confirm CDP is running on that port.

states: Neighbor not in show cdp neighbors
Port listed? → problem is on the other side
Port missing? → no cdp enable or down

hack.memory: show cdp interface = per-port CDP status. If a port is missing, CDP is off on it. First stop when troubleshooting 'missing neighbor.' The command to narrow to one port: show cdp interface Gi0/1.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: show cdp interface on a switch — note all ports listed. Run no cdp enable on one interface. Re-run — that port is gone from the output. This is the instant visual for 'CDP is off on this port.'
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 1 question at most, usually in a troubleshooting context.

2.3.b.info:
show cdp entry [device-id] and its wildcard variant show cdp entry * return detail output for a specific neighbor by hostname (or all neighbors). It is essentially show cdp neighbors detail filtered to one device, which is handy on large networks where the full detail output is dozens of pages.

Syntax: show cdp entry SW2 shows the full detail block for the neighbor whose Device ID is SW2. Case-insensitive. You can use partial matches in some IOS versions with wildcards. show cdp entry * shows detail for every neighbor — equivalent to show cdp neighbors detail.

When to use it: You saw SW3 in the basic neighbors table and want just its detail — don't scroll through the entire detail output. show cdp entry SW3 returns the single block. Also useful in scripts and TCL automation where you want to grep for one neighbor without parsing multi-block output.

Exam framing: This is a low-weight command — you likely won't see it in the exam directly, but it could appear as a distractor answer choice. Know it exists and what it does, but prioritize the neighbors/detail commands first.

Protocol-filtered variant: show cdp entry [device-id] protocol shows only the protocol/address TLVs for that neighbor (e.g., IP addresses). show cdp entry [device-id] version shows only the software version. These narrow the output even further when you need one specific field.

Output identical to detail: When run against a single device, the output format matches show cdp neighbors detail exactly — same fields, same structure, just filtered.

children.name: show cdp entry [id] protocol (one, IP only)

hack.memory: show cdp entry [hostname] = 'show me detail for THIS one neighbor.' Same info as detail but targeted. Useful on 50-port distribution switches where full detail is huge.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: with 3+ CDP neighbors, run show cdp entry * (all) vs show cdp entry SW2 (one). Compare output lengths — targeted entry is 1/N the size.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 mentions it briefly. OCG Chapter 11. Low exam weight — recognize it as a valid command if it appears as a distractor.

2.3.c.info:
cdp run is the global configuration command that enables CDP on the entire device. Because CDP is enabled by default on all Cisco devices, you will almost never need to type this command on a fresh system — it's already running. The command's real usefulness is reversing a previous no cdp run.

Syntax and context:

Router(config)# cdp run        ! enable CDP globally (default)
Router(config)# no cdp run     ! disable CDP on the entire device

Only valid in global config mode (configure terminal). When disabled globally, every CDP neighbor disappears from the table and no CDP frames are sent or received on any interface — regardless of per-interface cdp enable settings. Global disable overrides everything.

When to use no cdp run: Rare, but valid for devices that sit entirely in untrusted zones — e.g., a router with only WAN-facing interfaces, or a DMZ device that should never reveal anything at Layer 2. Most hardening guides prefer per-interface control (no cdp enable) instead, because it keeps CDP available for internal troubleshooting.

Verification: show cdp. If CDP is running, you see the timer and holdtime. If not, you see '% CDP is not enabled' or 'CDP is not enabled'.

Order-of-operations rule: no cdp run (global) wins over cdp enable (interface). If CDP is disabled globally, per-port enable does nothing. To fully run CDP on even one port, you need BOTH: global cdp run AND interface cdp enable. Both are defaults, which is why CDP 'just works' out of the box.

Exam framing: 'Which command enables CDP globally?' → cdp run. 'Which command disables CDP on the entire device?' → no cdp run. Do not confuse with the interface command cdp enable — the exam tests this distinction directly.

children.name: Global: cdp run / no cdp run
Interface: cdp enable / no cdp enable

hack.memory: 'Run is global.' cdp run = turn CDP on for the whole box (global config). The 'no' form kills CDP everywhere. Default = running. Exam trap: 'run' sounds like interface-level but it's NOT — it's global.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: verify CDP is running (show cdp). Disable globally: no cdp run. Every neighbor disappears from show cdp neighbors. Re-enable: cdp run. Neighbors return within 60 seconds.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 1 recall question, usually paired with cdp enable to test global vs interface distinction.

2.3.c.info:
cdp enable is the interface configuration command that enables CDP on a specific port. Default state is enabled, so you rarely type cdp enable — its real value is undoing a previous no cdp enable.

Syntax:

Switch(config)# interface Gi0/1
Switch(config-if)# cdp enable         ! enable CDP on this port (default)
Switch(config-if)# no cdp enable      ! disable CDP on this port only

Valid only under an interface context. Affects only the interface where it is applied. Other interfaces remain at their own CDP state.

Interplay with cdp run: For CDP to actually run on a port, BOTH conditions must be true: global CDP is on (cdp run, default) AND the interface has cdp enable (default). If either is off, the port does not send CDP. If global CDP is off (no cdp run), per-interface cdp enable is ignored — global always wins.

Security use case: The canonical hardening pattern is 'global on, external ports off.' You keep cdp run enabled so internal troubleshooting works, and run no cdp enable on every interface that leaves the trust boundary (internet WAN, DMZ, guest VLAN ports, lobby kiosks, contractor ports). This is the default recommendation in Cisco's own hardening guide.

Verification: show cdp interface — enabled interfaces appear; disabled interfaces are absent from the list. Alternatively, show running-config interface Gi0/1 will include no cdp enable if CDP was explicitly disabled on that port.

Exam framing: 'Which command disables CDP on a specific port?' → no cdp enable. 'Which command is entered in interface config to turn CDP back on?' → cdp enable. Do not confuse with global cdp run. The word 'enable' is interface-level; the word 'run' is global.

hack.memory: Two-word memory key: 'Run = global, Enable = interface.' The verb tells you the scope. cdp run under (config)#. cdp enable under (config-if)#. Never reverse them.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: interface Gi0/1 → no cdp enable. show cdp interface — Gi0/1 is gone. Within 180s, neighbor's entry for your port expires. Re-enable — entry returns in 60s.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: guaranteed question on global vs interface-level command distinction.

2.3.c.info:
cdp timer [seconds] is a global configuration command that changes the CDP advertisement interval. Default is 60 seconds. Valid range is 5–254 seconds.

Why tune it: In most networks the default is fine — CDP is a management convenience, not a fast-convergence protocol. Lower the timer only when faster neighbor detection matters (e.g., automation pipelines that detect topology changes, or environments where you want CDP entries to reflect reality within seconds). Raise it on CPU-constrained devices or in environments where CDP traffic itself is a concern (rare).

Tradeoffs: Lower interval = faster convergence, more CPU, more bandwidth. Higher interval = slower convergence, less overhead. A 60-second default balances both and is what the exam expects.

Configuration:

Switch(config)# cdp timer 30    ! advertise every 30 seconds
Switch(config)# cdp timer 60    ! back to default

Applies globally — all interfaces inherit. There is no per-interface timer override in CDP (unlike OSPF hello timers, which can be per-interface).

Relationship to holdtime: The holdtime should always be larger than the timer — typically 3x, the Cisco default pattern. If you lower the timer to 10 seconds, consider lowering the holdtime to 30 (cdp holdtime 30) to maintain the 3:1 ratio. Setting holdtime < timer means neighbors expire before the next advertisement arrives — nonsensical.

Verification: show cdp displays the current timer and holdtime. show cdp interface shows the effective timer per port.

Exam framing: Know the default is 60 seconds and that the command is global. 'Which command changes the CDP advertisement interval?' → cdp timer [seconds]. Low frequency on the exam but could appear as a distractor.

hack.memory: cdp timer = global, sets the send interval. Default 60s. Range 5–254. Pair with cdp holdtime using the 3x rule. If timer is 10, holdtime should be ~30.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: cdp timer 20 globally. Watch Holdtme in show cdp neighbors reset every 20 seconds instead of 60. Undo: cdp timer 60.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 mentions timer tuning briefly. OCG Chapter 11. Low-weight but valid exam fact: default 60s, globally configured.

2.3.c.info:
cdp holdtime [seconds] is the global configuration command that sets how long a received CDP advertisement remains valid before it expires. Default is 180 seconds. Valid range is 10–255 seconds.

How it works: When a CDP frame is received, the holdtime field in that frame tells the receiver how long to keep the entry. By default, Cisco devices send CDP frames with a holdtime of 180 seconds — the receiver stores the neighbor and starts a countdown from 180. Each subsequent CDP frame resets the counter. If 180 seconds pass without a refresh, the neighbor entry is deleted.

The 3x rule: Default holdtime is 3x the advertisement timer (60 × 3 = 180). This gives two 'grace' windows — you can lose up to two consecutive advertisements (120 seconds of silence) before the entry expires. If the ratio were 1x, a single dropped frame would flush the neighbor, causing table churn. 3x is a classic network-protocol safety cushion and is used in many protocols (OSPF dead = 4x hello, HSRP hold = 3x hello, etc.).

Configuration:

Switch(config)# cdp holdtime 60     ! shorter — entries expire faster
Switch(config)# cdp holdtime 180    ! default

The Holdtme column in show cdp neighbors: This displays the remaining seconds on the current entry, not the configured holdtime. Each received frame resets it to whatever holdtime the neighbor advertised. If neighbor A is configured with holdtime 300, and neighbor B with default 180, their Holdtme columns in your show output will differ.

Scope: This is a global command; all interfaces inherit. The value sent in outbound frames equals the locally configured holdtime. The value stored for received neighbors equals the holdtime the sender advertised.

Exam framing: Default 180 seconds. Global config. 3:1 ratio with timer. 'Which command changes how long CDP entries remain valid?' → cdp holdtime [seconds].

hack.memory: Holdtime = 'how long until I forget you' = 180s default = 3x the send timer. The Holdtme column in show cdp neighbors counts DOWN — not the configured value. Pair with timer using 3x rule.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: cdp holdtime 60. Check show cdp neighbors — Holdtme column now starts at 60 and resets every 60 seconds. Pull the cable — entry expires in 60s instead of 180s.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: memorize default 180s and the 3x-timer ratio.

2.3.c.info:
CDP's default-on behavior creates a passive reconnaissance surface on every Cisco device. An attacker who can plug into or MITM a CDP-enabled port receives, every 60 seconds and without sending a single packet, a rich profile of the device they are attached to.

What leaks:

  • Hostname — reveals naming scheme, often maps to location or function (e.g., SW-CORE-01-DALLAS).
  • Management IP address — pivots the attacker from L2 to L3 targeting; they now know where to aim SSH/SNMP/HTTPS probes.
  • Platform — hardware model. Maps to feature set and performance class.
  • IOS/NX-OS version — matches directly to CVE databases. A known-vulnerable version like 12.2(55)SE1 instantly becomes exploitable with published tools.
  • Native VLAN — critical for VLAN-hopping attacks (double-encapsulation/DTP attacks). If native is VLAN 1 and management traffic rides it, attackers can craft frames to reach management.
  • VTP domain — enables VTP spoofing if the attacker can connect as a trunk.
  • Duplex, interface name, capability flags — additional fingerprint data.

Real-world attacks: Penetration testing tools like Yersinia and Cain automate CDP frame parsing. Within 60 seconds of connecting a laptop to a corporate port with default CDP, the tester has hostname + IP + exact IOS version — enough to pick a targeted exploit from Metasploit's Cisco modules. This is a documented standard first step in physical-access penetration tests.

Mitigation layers: (1) no cdp enable on every untrusted-facing interface. (2) no cdp run globally on devices in fully-hostile zones (e.g., an internet-edge router with no internal users). (3) 802.1X port authentication to prevent unauthorized devices from connecting in the first place. (4) Use LLDP only where CDP interop is not required (LLDP leaks similar info but is at least industry-standard and more commonly filtered).

Exam framing: 'What information does CDP leak that constitutes a security risk?' → hostname, IP, platform, IOS version. 'What is the best practice for CDP on an internet-facing interface?' → no cdp enable. These are direct recall questions worth a point each.

params.label: CDP leaks in a 60-second window
params.defense: no cdp enable on untrusted ports

hack.memory: CDP leaks = 'HIP-V NV' — Hostname, IP, Platform, Version, Native VLAN, VTP domain. Passive. 60-second interval. Attacker gets it all just by plugging in. Fix: no cdp enable on every external port.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: connect a laptop to a switch with default CDP. Use Wireshark (simulated) to capture — you see the CDP frame with all fields in cleartext. Now run no cdp enable on that port. No more CDP frames. Security hardened.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 covers the security angle. OCG Chapter 11. Cisco's hardening guide and CIS benchmarks both mandate no cdp enable on external-facing interfaces. Exam: 1 recall question on what CDP reveals and why that's a risk.

2.3.d.info:
Link Layer Discovery Protocol (LLDP) is the IEEE-standardized, vendor-neutral counterpart to CDP. Defined in IEEE 802.1AB, LLDP does the same job as CDP — announcing and learning about directly-connected neighbors at Layer 2 — but works between any vendor's equipment. Cisco, Arista, Juniper, HPE, Dell, Huawei, and every other major network vendor implements LLDP.

Why LLDP exists: CDP is Cisco-only. In the 1990s, when nearly every enterprise was all-Cisco, that was fine. By the mid-2000s, networks mixed vendors — data centers had Juniper routers next to Cisco switches next to Arista top-of-rack boxes, and they all needed to discover each other. The IEEE standardized LLDP (802.1AB) in 2005 to solve this, and it has been the default discovery protocol for non-Cisco vendors ever since. Cisco added LLDP support to IOS around the same time but kept CDP as the default for backward compatibility.

Frame format: LLDP uses an Ethernet frame with destination MAC 01:80:C2:00:00:0E — one of the IEEE-reserved 'nearest bridge' addresses. This MAC is specifically designed not to cross 802.1D bridges, enforcing LLDP's one-hop scope at the hardware level. EtherType is 0x88CC. The payload is a sequence of TLV (Type-Length-Value) fields.

Capability parity with CDP: LLDP advertises essentially the same information as CDP — system name, port ID, management address, system capabilities, TTL, and optional fields like VLAN, PoE, and vendor-specific extensions. The differences are in framing, defaults, and granularity, not capability.

Relationship to Cisco networks: On a modern mixed-vendor network, you often run both CDP and LLDP simultaneously. Cisco devices talk CDP to each other and LLDP to non-Cisco devices. Both run independently; they do not interfere. Many production networks simply enable both everywhere for consistent visibility.

Exam framing: 'Which L2 discovery protocol is vendor-neutral?' → LLDP. 'What is the IEEE standard for LLDP?' → 802.1AB. These are fast recall questions.

hack.memory: LLDP = 'Loves aLL Devices Possible' (vendor-neutral). IEEE 802.1AB. EtherType 0x88CC. Multicast MAC 01:80:C2:00:00:0E. The key exam hook is IEEE = open standard, which means multi-vendor.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, connect a Cisco switch to any 'Generic' device with LLDP. Enable lldp run globally on the Cisco side. Run show lldp neighbors — the generic device appears because LLDP is cross-vendor. CDP could not see it.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 1 direct question on LLDP as the IEEE standard. Pair the 802.1AB number with the EtherType 0x88CC for full credit on any 'which is LLDP?' distractor question.

2.3.d.info:
On Cisco devices, LLDP is disabled by default. This is the direct opposite of CDP (enabled by default). It is the single most-tested CDP-vs-LLDP fact on the CCNA, so if you learn nothing else about these two protocols, learn this.

Why the opposite default: Cisco prioritizes its proprietary protocol (CDP) because it carries Cisco-specific extras (VTP domain, native VLAN as separate TLVs, richer capability codes). LLDP is available but off until the admin explicitly enables it. This preserves default behavior for the large base of all-Cisco customers while allowing easy opt-in for multi-vendor environments.

How to enable:

Switch(config)# lldp run     ! enable LLDP globally
Switch(config)# no lldp run  ! disable LLDP globally (default state)

Once enabled, every interface begins sending LLDP frames (at the 30-second default interval) and begins listening for them. You can then refine with per-interface lldp transmit and lldp receive commands.

Verification: show lldp. If LLDP is running, you see the timer, holdtime, and reinit delay. If not, the command returns 'LLDP is not enabled'.

Dual-stack operation: Enabling LLDP does not disable CDP. Both can run simultaneously on the same device — they are independent protocols using different MACs and EtherTypes. This is common in practice: enable LLDP globally so non-Cisco neighbors appear, leave CDP enabled so Cisco neighbors appear, done.

Exam framing: This fact appears in nearly every CCNA test form. 'Which of the following is disabled by default on Cisco devices?' → LLDP. 'Which command enables LLDP globally?' → lldp run. Memorize the opposite-defaults pair and you'll never miss a CDP/LLDP default-state question.

hack.memory: CDP = ON by default. LLDP = OFF by default. Opposite defaults. Enable LLDP with lldp run globally — EXACT mirror of cdp run. This is the #1 CCNA gotcha on L2 discovery.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: fresh switch. show cdp → running. show lldp → 'LLDP is not enabled'. Run lldp run. show lldp again → running with timers. Your mental snapshot: CDP on from birth, LLDP waiting for a command.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 1–2 questions hinge on this. Pair with CDP's default-on state. Always answer: CDP=on, LLDP=off.

2.3.d.info:
LLDP uses the same timer/holdtime pattern as CDP, but with faster defaults: 30-second advertisement interval and 120-second holdtime. That is a 4:1 ratio (vs CDP's 3:1 at 60/180). The faster advertisement means LLDP detects topology changes more quickly than CDP.

The three LLDP timers:

  • timer (advertisement interval) — default 30 seconds. Range 5–32768. Configured with lldp timer [seconds] globally.
  • holdtime — default 120 seconds. Actually configured as a multiplier of the timer, but the exam treats it as an absolute value. Command: lldp holdtime [seconds].
  • reinit (reinitialization delay) — default 2 seconds. The delay before LLDP restarts after being disabled and re-enabled on an interface. Command: lldp reinit [seconds]. This timer has no CDP equivalent and is specific to LLDP.

Why faster than CDP: LLDP was standardized later and designed for modern high-speed networks where sub-minute convergence matters (e.g., data-center fabrics, VoIP deployments that rely on LLDP-MED). 30/120 reflects that faster-pace design. CDP's 60/180 is legacy from 1990s LAN speeds.

Configuration example:

Switch(config)# lldp timer 10     ! ad every 10 seconds
Switch(config)# lldp holdtime 40  ! 4x the timer
Switch(config)# lldp reinit 2     ! default

Verification: show lldp displays all three timers. show lldp interface shows per-port state.

Exam framing: The timer pair (30/120) is a guaranteed exam fact when paired with CDP's (60/180). Memorize both pairs side-by-side. 'What are the default LLDP timers?' → 30 seconds (ad) and 120 seconds (hold). 'Which protocol has faster default timers, CDP or LLDP?' → LLDP (2x faster).

hack.memory: LLDP = 30/120 (and 2s reinit). CDP = 60/180. LLDP is exactly 2x faster. Mnemonic: 'LLDP Loves Low timers.' Four numbers to memorize total: 60, 180, 30, 120. Write them on your exam whiteboard FIRST.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer with lldp run enabled: watch the Holdtme column in show lldp neighbors — it resets to 120 every 30 seconds. Pull the cable, watch the entry expire at the 120-second mark. Compare to CDP's 180-second expiry on the same setup.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11 includes a timer comparison table. Exam: guaranteed timer-pair question. Pair CDP (60/180) and LLDP (30/120) in every flashcard.

2.3.d.info:
LLDP carries information in TLVs (Type-Length-Value) — a flexible, extensible encoding where each field is tagged with a type code, a length, and the data itself. This design lets IEEE, vendors, and consortiums add new TLVs without breaking existing implementations. Every LLDP frame is a concatenation of TLVs terminated by an 'End of LLDPDU' TLV.

Mandatory TLVs (always present in every LLDPDU):

  • Chassis ID (type 1) — uniquely identifies the sending chassis. Usually the chassis MAC address.
  • Port ID (type 2) — identifies the sending port (interface name or MAC).
  • TTL (type 3) — Time To Live in seconds; this is LLDP's holdtime field. Default 120.
  • End of LLDPDU (type 0) — marks the end of the frame.

Optional 'basic management' TLVs (almost always sent):

  • Port Description (type 4) — the human-readable description configured with description on the interface.
  • System Name (type 5) — the device hostname.
  • System Description (type 6) — a full string like 'Cisco IOS Software, C2960 Software, Version 15.0(2)SE11'.
  • System Capabilities (type 7) — flag bits indicating bridge, router, WLAN AP, telephone, etc.
  • Management Address (type 8) — the device's management IP (IPv4 or IPv6). This is the field that answers 'what is the neighbor's IP?' in show lldp neighbors detail.

Organizational TLVs (extensibility): IEEE 802.1 (VLAN info), 802.3 (PoE, link aggregation), and vendor-specific (Cisco, Microsoft, etc.) can all define their own TLVs. LLDP-MED (Media Endpoint Discovery) is a set of TIA-standardized TLVs used heavily for VoIP phone provisioning — voice VLAN, PoE class, inventory.

CDP parallel: CDP also uses TLVs internally, but the set is fixed and Cisco-defined. LLDP's extensibility via organizational TLVs is a key differentiator — anyone can register a vendor-specific TLV without asking Cisco.

Exam framing: Know that LLDP uses TLVs. Know the core mandatory and management TLVs (Chassis ID, Port ID, TTL, System Name, Management Address, System Capabilities). 'What encoding format does LLDP use?' → TLV.

layers: TLV: System Name, Description, Capabilities, Mgmt Addr (optional but usually sent)

hack.memory: TLV = Type, Length, Value. Flexible and extensible. Mandatory TLVs: Chassis ID, Port ID, TTL, End. Almost-always-sent: System Name, Description, Capabilities, Management Address. If asked what LLDP uses for info encoding → TLV.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: show lldp neighbors detail on a live LLDP adjacency. Each field in the output corresponds to a TLV. Match them: System Name = type 5, Management Address = type 8, etc. Real Wireshark capture of LLDP shows the actual TLV bytes.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 mentions TLVs. OCG Chapter 11 lists the mandatory set. Exam: medium-weight recall — know that LLDP uses TLVs and name 3–5 core types.

2.3.d.info:
LLDP's separate transmit and receive control per interface is its single biggest feature-level difference from CDP. Each port has two independent switches: lldp transmit (do I announce myself?) and lldp receive (do I listen for neighbors?). You can enable one and not the other. CDP has only a single toggle — cdp enable / no cdp enable — that controls both directions together.

The four combinations per interface:

  • Both on (default when LLDP is enabled globally): port sends and receives LLDP. Full bidirectional discovery.
  • Receive only (no lldp transmit, lldp receive): port listens to neighbors but does not announce itself. Useful on monitoring ports or security-sensitive interfaces where you want to learn but not leak.
  • Transmit only (lldp transmit, no lldp receive): port announces itself but ignores incoming LLDP. Rare. Sometimes used for phone provisioning where the phone needs to learn the switch but the switch doesn't need to record the phone.
  • Both off (no lldp transmit, no lldp receive): effectively LLDP disabled on this port.

Why this matters: CDP forces you to choose 'all or nothing' per port. LLDP lets you do 'learn without reveal,' which is a common security posture: the NMS wants to know what's plugged in, but doesn't want to broadcast its own details to whatever plugged in.

Configuration:

Switch(config)# interface Gi0/1
Switch(config-if)# no lldp transmit   ! stop announcing
Switch(config-if)# lldp receive       ! keep listening

Verification: show lldp interface Gi0/1 displays the transmit and receive states independently. You'll see lines like 'Tx: enabled' and 'Rx: enabled' — or one can be 'disabled' while the other remains 'enabled.'

Exam framing: This is the #1 LLDP-vs-CDP feature difference on the exam. 'Which protocol supports independent transmit and receive control per interface?' → LLDP. 'What command disables only LLDP transmission on a port?' → no lldp transmit.

hack.memory: LLDP = 'Listen and Loud-speak separately.' Two knobs: transmit (mouth) and receive (ears). You can turn off your mouth and keep ears on — that's receive-only mode, great for security. CDP has ONE knob. If the exam asks which protocol has separate TX/RX → always LLDP.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: enable LLDP globally. On Gi0/1: no lldp transmit. Verify with show lldp interface Gi0/1 — Tx shows disabled, Rx shows enabled. Your neighbor loses you in their table (you stopped announcing), but you still see them. Now reverse: lldp transmit and no lldp receive. Neighbor sees you again; you stop recording them.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 demonstrates this. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: guaranteed comparison question. LLDP = separate TX/RX; CDP = single toggle. This is the most-tested feature difference after the default-state pair.

2.3.e.info:
show lldp neighbors is the LLDP summary command, structurally identical to show cdp neighbors but with LLDP-specific capability codes. It produces a multi-column table with one row per discovered LLDP neighbor.

Columns (LLDP version): Device ID (neighbor's System Name), Local Intf (your local port — note the abbreviation is slightly different from CDP's 'Local Intrfce'), Hold-time (seconds remaining), Capability (LLDP codes — see below), Port ID (neighbor's port).

LLDP capability codes (differ from CDP):

  • B — Bridge (a Layer 2 switch — note CDP uses 'S' for Switch).
  • R — Router (same letter as CDP).
  • T — Telephone (IP phone — note CDP uses 'H' for Host).
  • W — WLAN Access Point.
  • D — DOCSIS cable device.
  • S — Station (an end-station, e.g., PC — note the letter overlap with CDP's Switch code, but the meaning here is different).
  • O — Other.
  • P — Repeater (rare).

The B-vs-S trap: A switch in CDP output shows 'S'. In LLDP output, a switch shows 'B' (Bridge). If you confuse them, you'll misidentify a device type. Memorize 'B = Bridge = Switch in LLDP.'

Sample output:

Switch# show lldp neighbors
Capability codes:
    (R) Router, (B) Bridge, (T) Telephone, (C) DOCSIS Cable Device
    (W) WLAN Access Point, (P) Repeater, (S) Station, (O) Other

Device ID        Local Intf    Hold-time  Capability      Port ID
SW2.example.com  Gi0/1         120        B               Gi0/24
AP-Lobby         Gi0/2         118        B,W             eth0
Phone-1001       Gi0/3         115        B,T             Port 1

Relationship to CDP: Structure is near-identical. Both summary tables, both ~5 columns, both one row per neighbor. The main differences: column names slightly vary (Local Intf vs Local Intrfce, Hold-time vs Holdtme), and capability codes use different letters.

Exam framing: Know the columns and the capability codes. 'What does B stand for in LLDP?' → Bridge (switch). Expect to be shown a show lldp neighbors output and asked to identify a neighbor's role.

hack.memory: show lldp neighbors = 5 columns, one row per neighbor. Capability codes: B=Bridge (LLDP's word for switch), R=Router, T=Telephone (not Host like CDP), W=WLAN AP. The B-vs-S swap is the #1 gotcha between CDP and LLDP capability codes.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: enable LLDP and run the command. Compare its output side-by-side with show cdp neighbors. Circle every difference — column names AND capability letters. Make a flashcard: 'In LLDP, a switch is code ___ (B, for Bridge).'
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 1 question on LLDP output parsing. Know B=Bridge for sure.

2.3.e.info:
show lldp neighbors detail is the deep-info form, the LLDP equivalent of show cdp neighbors detail. It reveals the fields hidden from the summary table — most importantly the Management Address (neighbor's IP).

What detail adds (vs basic):

  • Management Address — the neighbor's IP, from the Management Address TLV. This is the headline field: same role as CDP detail's IP.
  • System Description — full hardware + software description string, e.g., 'Cisco IOS Software, C2960 Software (C2960-LANBASEK9-M), Version 15.0(2)SE11'.
  • System Capabilities — supported vs enabled — LLDP distinguishes capabilities a device supports from those currently enabled. A router may support bridge mode but have it disabled, and the TLV reflects that.
  • Port Description — the human-readable description from the neighbor's description command on that port.
  • Additional optional TLVs — VLAN ID, PoE info, LLDP-MED fields (for VoIP phones), any vendor-specific extensions.

Sample detail block:

Switch# show lldp neighbors detail
------------------------------------------------
Chassis id: 0011.2233.4455
Port id: Gi0/24
Port Description: Uplink to SW1
System Name: SW2.example.com
System Description:
  Cisco IOS Software, C2960 Software...
Time remaining: 112 seconds
System Capabilities: B
Enabled Capabilities: B
Management Addresses:
  IP: 10.1.1.2
Auto Negotiation: supported, enabled
Physical media capabilities: ...
VLAN ID: 1

Relationship to CDP detail: Near-identical in purpose and content. Both reveal the neighbor's management IP. Both include the software version string. LLDP adds the supported-vs-enabled capability distinction and the LLDP-MED extensions; CDP adds native VLAN and VTP domain as distinct TLVs.

Exam framing: 'How do you find the IP of an LLDP neighbor?' → show lldp neighbors detail. 'What command gives full system description for LLDP?' → same. Expect 1 exam question that is the exact LLDP mirror of the CDP detail question.

hack.memory: LLDP detail = IP + full version + capability split (supported vs enabled) + port description. Mirrors CDP detail. Golden rule applies to BOTH protocols: IP address only shows up in detail.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: with LLDP running, run both summary and detail commands on the same switch. Highlight the fields detail adds. Do the same for CDP. Build a 2-column flashcard: 'CDP detail adds' / 'LLDP detail adds' — the shapes match.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11 has sample detail outputs. Exam: 1–2 questions parsing LLDP detail output. The IP is always the headline field.

2.3.e.info:
LLDP uses a different set of single-letter capability codes than CDP. Mixing them up on the exam is a fast way to lose points. The codes come from the IEEE 802.1AB standard and are consistent across every LLDP vendor.

LLDP capability codes:

  • B — Bridge — any Layer 2 bridge or switch. This is the LLDP word for 'switch.' Note the difference from CDP, which uses S for Switch.
  • R — Router — Layer 3 router or multilayer switch with routing enabled. Same letter as CDP.
  • T — Telephone — an IP phone. Note the difference from CDP (H for Host).
  • W — WLAN Access Point — a wireless AP. No direct CDP equivalent.
  • D (or C) — DOCSIS Cable Device — cable modems and related DOCSIS gear. Rare in enterprise CCNA scope.
  • S — Station Only — an end-station (PC, server). Confusingly this letter means 'Switch' in CDP — do not mix them up.
  • O — Other — any device type not covered above.
  • P — Repeater — legacy hub behavior. Rare.

Multiple codes: Like CDP, LLDP capabilities are not mutually exclusive. A multilayer switch advertises 'B,R' (Bridge + Router). A phone with a built-in mini-switch for a PC daisy chain advertises 'B,T'. A wireless AP with a built-in switch advertises 'B,W'.

Supported vs enabled: LLDP distinguishes capabilities a device is capable of (supported) from those it currently has turned on (enabled). A router that supports bridging but has it disabled would show 'B,R' as supported and 'R' as enabled. CDP does not have this distinction — it only shows enabled capabilities.

Most common CDP ↔ LLDP mappings:

  • CDP 'S' (Switch) ↔ LLDP 'B' (Bridge)
  • CDP 'H' (Host, often IP phone) ↔ LLDP 'T' (Telephone) or 'S' (Station)
  • CDP 'R' (Router) ↔ LLDP 'R' (Router) — same letter

Exam framing: 'What does B mean in LLDP output?' → Bridge (switch). 'Which LLDP code identifies an IP phone?' → T. Know these cold.

hack.memory: LLDP codes: 'BRT-WD-SOP' = Bridge, Router, Telephone, WLAN, DOCSIS, Station, Other, Repeater. Key swap vs CDP: B (not S) = switch, T (not H) = phone. If you remember only one swap, remember B=Bridge=Switch in LLDP.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: connect a switch, router, phone, and PC. Run show lldp neighbors and match each device to its capability code. Then show cdp neighbors and compare — note which letters differ (S↔B, H↔T).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 1 question on LLDP codes, usually contrasted with CDP codes. Know the B-is-Bridge mapping.

2.3.e.info:
show lldp interface displays per-interface LLDP state with the protocol's signature feature: independent transmit and receive status. Unlike show cdp interface (which treats CDP as on/off per port), this command lists Tx and Rx separately — so you can see a port that is listening but not announcing, or vice versa.

Typical output:

Switch# show lldp interface
GigabitEthernet0/1:
  Tx: enabled
  Rx: enabled
  Tx state: IDLE
  Rx state: WAIT FOR FRAME

GigabitEthernet0/2:
  Tx: enabled
  Rx: disabled
  Tx state: IDLE
  Rx state: DISABLED

The Tx/Rx lines are the whole point. For each interface, you see two status fields instead of one. If you configured no lldp transmit on Gi0/2 but left lldp receive, the output shows 'Tx: disabled, Rx: enabled' — exactly the state you set.

Narrow to one port: show lldp interface Gi0/1 shows just that interface. Useful on large switches.

Troubleshooting use: When a neighbor is missing from show lldp neighbors, check this command first to confirm the port is at least configured to transmit and receive. If it shows 'Tx: disabled' you know the port is not announcing — the neighbor can't see you. If 'Rx: disabled' the port is deaf.

Relationship to CDP: show cdp interface just lists ports where CDP is on (or omits them if off). Simpler output because CDP has only one toggle. LLDP's Tx/Rx split justifies the richer output.

Exam framing: Know that this command exists and that it reports Tx and Rx state independently. 'Which command verifies that LLDP is both sending and receiving on a port?' → show lldp interface.

states: Port: both disabled (LLDP off on this port)

hack.memory: show lldp interface = Tx and Rx on separate lines per port. Mirrors the protocol's feature (independent transmit/receive). If CDP output says 'enabled/disabled' as one line, LLDP says it on two lines.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: lldp run. show lldp interface — all ports show Tx: enabled, Rx: enabled. Run no lldp transmit on Gi0/1. Re-run — Gi0/1 now reads Tx: disabled, Rx: enabled. This 30-second visual makes the separate TX/RX feature click.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: low-weight but legitimate. Pair with lldp transmit and lldp receive commands.

2.3.f.info:
lldp run is the global configuration command that enables LLDP on the entire device. Because LLDP is disabled by default on Cisco, this command is not optional — you must type it to use LLDP. This is the opposite of CDP, where cdp run is already the default.

Syntax:

Switch(config)# lldp run        ! enable LLDP globally
Switch(config)# no lldp run     ! disable LLDP globally (the default state)

Global config only. After lldp run, every interface automatically begins transmitting and receiving LLDP with defaults (Tx: enabled, Rx: enabled). To fine-tune, use the per-interface lldp transmit and lldp receive commands.

Verification: show lldp — if LLDP is running, it prints the timer (30s), holdtime (120s), and reinit delay (2s). If not, it says 'LLDP is not enabled.' show running-config | include lldp confirms whether the command is in the config.

Coexistence with CDP: Enabling LLDP does not disable CDP. Both protocols can run simultaneously on every port and are independent. Many production networks enable both globally to handle mixed-vendor neighbors gracefully.

The parallel to CDP: lldp run is the exact mirror of cdp run, just for a different protocol. The command structure is intentionally parallel so engineers familiar with one protocol's config can pick up the other quickly.

Exam framing: 'Which command enables LLDP globally?' → lldp run. 'Why is lldp run needed but cdp run is not?' → LLDP is off by default; CDP is on by default. Direct recall — expect 1 question.

hack.memory: LLDP is OFF by default — must run lldp run. Global config. The exact mirror of cdp run. Remember: 'Run is always global' for both protocols.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: verify LLDP off (show lldp → not enabled). Run lldp run. Wait 30 seconds. show lldp neighbors — adjacencies appear. Remove: no lldp run. Neighbors disappear instantly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: guaranteed 1 question, typically paired with the default-state distinction vs CDP.

2.3.f.info:
lldp transmit is the interface configuration command that controls whether a port sends LLDP advertisements. Default is enabled once LLDP is running globally. The no form disables transmission on that specific port while leaving lldp receive unaffected.

Syntax:

Switch(config)# interface Gi0/1
Switch(config-if)# lldp transmit        ! send LLDP from this port (default)
Switch(config-if)# no lldp transmit     ! stop sending LLDP on this port only

What it controls: Whether LLDP frames originate from this port. If disabled, the port does not announce itself — neighbors on the other end will not see this device in their LLDP table. The port can still receive frames (see lldp receive), so you can still learn about your neighbor. This asymmetric behavior is impossible with CDP.

Use case: security monitoring port. On a port that monitors a sensitive network, you may want to identify who is plugged in (receive) without revealing the monitoring device itself (no transmit). Configure lldp receive + no lldp transmit and the port becomes a silent listener.

Use case: selective announce. On a port that connects to a phone but where you do not want the phone's host/PC daisy-chain port to discover the switch, you can disable transmit to reduce information leakage.

Interaction with global LLDP: lldp transmit does nothing if LLDP is not globally enabled. Both lldp run (global) AND lldp transmit (interface) must be in place to actually send frames. The CDP parallel would be cdp run + cdp enable — but CDP only has one interface knob, not a separate TX knob.

Verification: show lldp interface [port] shows 'Tx: enabled' or 'Tx: disabled' on its own line, independently from Rx state.

Exam framing: 'Which command disables only LLDP transmission on a specific port?' → no lldp transmit. 'Can you configure a port to receive but not send LLDP?' → Yes — that is the defining feature of LLDP vs CDP.

states: no lldp transmit → Tx disabled (but Rx still on)

hack.memory: lldp transmit = 'mouth on.' Interface-level. Default on when global is on. no lldp transmit = silent announcer — port still listens (Rx) but stops telling neighbors about itself. CDP has no equivalent.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer with LLDP running: on Gi0/1, no lldp transmit. From the neighbor's side, the neighbor's show lldp neighbors shows the entry age out in 120s. From your side, you still see the neighbor — proof of the asymmetric behavior.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 1 direct question; possibly in a 'which protocol allows this?' comparison.

2.3.f.info:
lldp receive is the interface configuration command that controls whether a port accepts and processes incoming LLDP frames. Default is enabled once LLDP is running globally. The no form disables reception on that port without affecting transmission.

Syntax:

Switch(config)# interface Gi0/1
Switch(config-if)# lldp receive         ! listen for LLDP on this port (default)
Switch(config-if)# no lldp receive      ! ignore incoming LLDP on this port

What it controls: Whether LLDP frames arriving on this port are processed and stored in the neighbor table. If disabled, the port is deaf — neighbor entries via this port will not populate in show lldp neighbors, regardless of what the other side sends. The port can still transmit — see lldp transmit.

Use case: broadcast-only port. If you want a device to announce itself but deliberately not learn about neighbors (e.g., for policy reasons, or to prevent an attacker's LLDP frame from being processed), configure lldp transmit + no lldp receive. Uncommon but legitimate.

Use case: avoiding neighbor pollution. On a port that faces many temporary devices (e.g., a test lab or a port that connects to a rogue-detection system), disabling receive prevents the neighbor table from filling up with ephemeral entries.

Interaction with global LLDP: Like transmit, lldp receive has no effect unless lldp run is also configured globally.

Verification: show lldp interface [port] — the 'Rx' line shows enabled or disabled independently from 'Tx.'

Exam framing: 'Which LLDP command prevents a port from processing incoming LLDP?' → no lldp receive. 'Can a port send but not receive LLDP?' → Yes. Pairs with lldp transmit in every CDP-vs-LLDP comparison question.

states: no lldp receive → Rx disabled (but Tx still on)

hack.memory: lldp receive = 'ears on.' Interface-level. no lldp receive = port is deaf to incoming LLDP but still announces itself if Tx is on. Pairs with lldp transmit. CDP has neither of these; it's a single toggle.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: on Gi0/1, no lldp receive. Still transmit. Neighbor still sees you. But you see nothing new on Gi0/1 — show lldp neighbors for that port is empty. Re-enable with lldp receive; neighbor entry reappears.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: complementary question to lldp transmit. Together they define LLDP's interface-level flexibility.

2.3.f.info:
A receive-only port combines no lldp transmit with lldp receive (enabled) to create an interface that learns about neighbors without announcing itself. This asymmetric configuration is unique to LLDP — CDP cannot do it.

Configuration:

Switch(config)# interface Gi0/24
Switch(config-if)# no lldp transmit
Switch(config-if)# lldp receive          ! (default; included for clarity)

Why use it: The primary rationale is security. Common scenarios:

  • Network monitoring appliance port — a SPAN destination or NMS tap that needs to know what is plugged into the network but should not reveal its own presence (the monitoring device may be on a restricted-disclosure list).
  • Rogue device detection — a port that records what connects to it but does not help the rogue device discover the switch.
  • DMZ sensor — an IDS/IPS sensor that listens for LLDP to catalog what's in the DMZ without leaking its own hostname and version.
  • Asset discovery without footprinting — during an audit, a team may want to identify connected devices without the audit device itself being visible in the neighbor's inventory.

Contrast with CDP: CDP's only option on an interface is 'on' or 'off.' There is no way to learn without announcing — if you want to listen you also broadcast. LLDP breaks this symmetry, which is why security-focused deployments often prefer LLDP over CDP on sensitive ports.

Verification: show lldp interface Gi0/24 shows 'Tx: disabled, Rx: enabled.' That pairing is the fingerprint of a receive-only port. Alternatively, show running-config interface Gi0/24 reveals the no lldp transmit line.

Exam framing: 'Which LLDP configuration listens for neighbors without announcing the device?' → no lldp transmit + lldp receive. 'Can CDP be configured this way?' → No — CDP uses a single enable/disable toggle per interface.

hack.memory: Receive-only = 'ears on, mouth off.' no lldp transmit + lldp receive. Learn without leaking. CDP can't do this — the ability to split Tx and Rx is LLDP's signature feature.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: on a switch port connected to another switch, configure receive-only. Verify with show lldp interface that Tx is disabled and Rx is enabled. From your side, you see the neighbor after ~30s. From the neighbor's side, your entry expires at 120s — they lose track of you.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 demonstrates this pattern. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: medium weight. Often phrased as 'which protocol / configuration lets a port learn neighbors without being learned?' → LLDP with receive-only.

2.3.f.info:
LLDP has three tunable global timers. Defaults are 30 seconds (timer / advertisement interval), 120 seconds (holdtime), and 2 seconds (reinit delay). The timer and holdtime mirror CDP's equivalents; reinit is unique to LLDP.

1. lldp timer [seconds]: Advertisement interval — how often LLDP frames are sent from every enabled port. Default 30. Range 5–32768. Command: lldp timer 30 (global).

2. lldp holdtime [seconds]: How long received LLDP entries remain valid. Default 120 (4x the timer — LLDP's standard ratio). Range is effectively bounded by the timer (must be > timer). Command: lldp holdtime 120 (global).

3. lldp reinit [seconds]: Reinitialization delay — the minimum wait time between disabling and re-enabling LLDP on an interface (to prevent rapid flapping). Default 2 seconds. Range 2–5. Command: lldp reinit 2 (global). This timer has no CDP equivalent.

Why three timers: The timer and holdtime serve the same role as in CDP — how often to advertise and how long an entry lives. Reinit is a protection against rapid re-enable cycles that could cause flooding of fresh LLDP frames. If an interface is disabled and re-enabled, LLDP waits 2 seconds before sending to let the link stabilize.

Tuning in practice: Defaults are suitable for the vast majority of networks. Lower timers (10s ad, 40s hold) can be used in fast-convergence environments — e.g., data-center fabrics that use LLDP for topology discovery by an SDN controller. Raise timers to reduce LLDP traffic on CPU-constrained devices.

Verification: show lldp displays all three timers. You'll see lines like 'Global LLDP Information: ... Sending frames every 30 seconds. Holdtime is 120 seconds. Reinit delay is 2 seconds.'

Exam framing: Know the three timer names and their defaults: 30s, 120s, 2s. 'Which timer is unique to LLDP?' → reinit. 'What is the default LLDP holdtime?' → 120 seconds.

hack.memory: LLDP 3 timers: 30 / 120 / 2. Timer = send interval (30s). Holdtime = keep entries (120s = 4x). Reinit = re-enable delay (2s, unique to LLDP). CDP has only 2 timers (60/180). The reinit is the exam curveball.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: show lldp — note all three timer values. Change timer to 10s (lldp timer 10), holdtime to 40s (lldp holdtime 40). Verify with show lldp. Watch show lldp neighbors — Hold-time now resets every 10s and expires at 40s.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 1 question on timer defaults. Know 30/120/2 and pair with CDP's 60/180.

2.3.g.info:
The foundational difference between CDP and LLDP is who owns the specification. CDP is Cisco-proprietary, meaning the protocol belongs to Cisco, is not published as an open standard, and cannot legally or technically be implemented by non-Cisco vendors (though third parties have reverse-engineered it). LLDP is IEEE 802.1AB, a published open standard that every major network vendor implements.

Implications of CDP being proprietary:

  • Only Cisco devices send or parse CDP. A Juniper switch next to a Cisco router will not show up in show cdp neighbors.
  • Cisco can evolve CDP at will — CDPv2 added features like VTP domain, native VLAN, and duplex without cross-vendor negotiation.
  • CDP is the default on all Cisco gear (60-second interval, on every port), giving Cisco a built-in management advantage in all-Cisco networks.

Implications of LLDP being IEEE 802.1AB:

  • Any compliant vendor — Arista, Juniper, HPE, Dell, Huawei, Mikrotik, etc. — can send and receive LLDP. It is the universal L2 discovery protocol.
  • Standards evolution is through IEEE working groups. Extensions like LLDP-MED (TIA-1057, for VoIP) go through standards processes, not single-vendor decisions.
  • LLDP is the discovery protocol of choice in multi-vendor data centers and enterprise networks.

Running both simultaneously: Cisco devices can run CDP and LLDP at the same time. In a mixed-vendor network, this is the standard practice — CDP handles Cisco-to-Cisco neighbors, LLDP handles Cisco-to-non-Cisco neighbors. Both populate independent neighbor tables accessible via their respective show commands.

Which one do other vendors advertise by default? Non-Cisco vendors enable LLDP by default. Cisco enables CDP by default. In a mixed network, if you want Cisco to see a Juniper neighbor, you must run lldp run on the Cisco side.

Exam framing: 'Which L2 discovery protocol is an IEEE open standard?' → LLDP. 'Which is Cisco-proprietary?' → CDP. 'What is the IEEE number for LLDP?' → 802.1AB. These are 1-point recall questions with near-100% presence.
