hack.memory: CDP = Cisco (proprietary). LLDP = IEEE 802.1AB (open). If the network is all-Cisco, CDP works fine. If it's multi-vendor, you need LLDP. Memorize the IEEE number: 802.1AB.
hack.practice: Lab it mentally: your switch is Cisco, neighbor is Arista. CDP shows nothing. Run lldp run on the Cisco side — after 30s the Arista appears in show lldp neighbors. This one pattern proves the vendor-neutrality difference.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 1 guaranteed recall question on standard type. Pair IEEE 802.1AB with LLDP on every flashcard.

2.3.g.info:
On Cisco devices, CDP is enabled by default and LLDP is disabled by default. This opposite-defaults pair is the single most-tested fact in Domain 2.3.

Why the opposite defaults: Cisco's business model historically rewarded ecosystem lock-in, and CDP being on-by-default means every Cisco device immediately integrates with Cisco-only management tools (Prime Infrastructure, DNA Center). LLDP is included for interop but left off to avoid advertising to non-Cisco neighbors by default. Other vendors' defaults are the reverse: they ship with LLDP on and CDP absent entirely.

How to change defaults:

  • Disable CDP globally: no cdp run.
  • Disable CDP on one port: no cdp enable (interface).
  • Enable LLDP globally: lldp run.
  • Per-interface LLDP (once global is on): lldp transmit and lldp receive are on by default; use no forms to disable.

Verification to confirm state:

  • show cdp — if running, shows timers; if not, says 'CDP is not enabled.'
  • show lldp — same pattern for LLDP.

Practical consequences: On a fresh Cisco switch, you see CDP neighbors immediately and LLDP neighbors not at all (until you run lldp run). Engineers new to mixed-vendor environments often miss this — they assume LLDP is on by default as it is on Arista/Juniper, and spend time troubleshooting a missing neighbor when the fix is a single global command.

Exam framing: This fact appears nearly verbatim on every CCNA test form. 'Which protocol is enabled by default on Cisco?' → CDP. 'Which protocol must be explicitly enabled?' → LLDP. 'What is the command?' → lldp run. Three variations of the same fact — know it cold.

states: Both CDP and LLDP now active

hack.memory: CDP = ON. LLDP = OFF. Opposite defaults on Cisco. Non-Cisco vendors reverse this. This one fact is the #1 exam hook for Domain 2.3 — if you blank on it you'll miss multiple questions.
hack.practice: Fresh Packet Tracer switch: show cdp (running), show lldp (not enabled). Toggle: no cdp run and lldp run. Reverse the defaults. Undo to restore. This 60-second lab cements the opposite-default behavior.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11 has a defaults table. Exam: 1–2 direct questions. Also tested via scenario questions ('why doesn't this Cisco switch see the Juniper neighbor?' → LLDP is off by default).

2.3.g.info:
CDP and LLDP use different default timer values. CDP sends every 60 seconds with a 180-second holdtime (3x ratio). LLDP sends every 30 seconds with a 120-second holdtime (4x ratio). LLDP is twice as fast at the advertisement level.

The four numbers to memorize:

  • CDP timer: 60 seconds
  • CDP holdtime: 180 seconds (3x timer)
  • LLDP timer: 30 seconds
  • LLDP holdtime: 120 seconds (4x timer)

Why LLDP is faster: LLDP was designed later, when networks had more CPU and bandwidth to spare, and where faster topology convergence mattered for SDN controllers and VoIP provisioning (LLDP-MED). CDP's 60-second default comes from 1990s LAN assumptions where frequent broadcasting was expensive. The 2x speed difference is deliberate, not accidental.

Ratio difference — 3x vs 4x: CDP uses holdtime = 3x timer. LLDP uses holdtime = 4x timer. Both follow the 'lose multiple advertisements before expiring' safety pattern, but LLDP gives one more grace window. Practical impact is minor but the exam tests the specific numbers.

Bonus: LLDP's unique reinit timer. LLDP adds a third timer — reinit delay, default 2 seconds, with no CDP equivalent. The reinit timer prevents rapid LLDP flapping when an interface is quickly disabled and re-enabled.

Both are tunable:

  • CDP: cdp timer [seconds], cdp holdtime [seconds].
  • LLDP: lldp timer [seconds], lldp holdtime [seconds], lldp reinit [seconds].

Exam framing: 'What are the default CDP timers?' → 60 / 180. 'What are the default LLDP timers?' → 30 / 120. 'Which protocol has a faster advertisement interval?' → LLDP. These appear as direct recall and as scenario questions ('the neighbor takes longer than 60 seconds to appear — which protocol is this?' → CDP).

hack.memory: The four-number chant: '60, 180, 30, 120.' Write it on the exam whiteboard FIRST. CDP = 60/180 (3x). LLDP = 30/120 (4x). LLDP is 2x faster. Bonus: LLDP has a reinit timer (2s); CDP does not.
hack.practice: Lab both protocols in Packet Tracer. Watch Holdtme in show cdp neighbors reset at 180s. Watch Hold-time in show lldp neighbors reset at 120s. Time the first advertisement to appear — 60s for CDP, 30s for LLDP. The difference is observable.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11 has the timer comparison table. Exam: guaranteed timer-pair question. The four numbers (60/180/30/120) are among the highest-yield memorizations in all of Domain 2.

2.3.g.info:
CDP offers a single per-interface toggle — the port is either on or off for CDP. LLDP offers two independent toggles — transmit and receive can each be enabled or disabled independently. This is the most frequently tested feature-level difference on the exam.

CDP interface control:

interface Gi0/1
 cdp enable        ! turn CDP on for this port (default)
 no cdp enable     ! turn CDP off for this port

One command, binary state. If CDP is on, the port both sends AND receives. If off, neither. There is no way to send without receiving (or vice versa) in CDP.

LLDP interface control:

interface Gi0/1
 lldp transmit     ! send LLDP (default when global is on)
 lldp receive      ! receive LLDP (default when global is on)
 no lldp transmit  ! stop sending on this port
 no lldp receive   ! stop receiving on this port

Two independent commands. You can:

  • Send and receive (default): both on.
  • Receive only: no lldp transmit + lldp receive.
  • Transmit only: lldp transmit + no lldp receive.
  • Neither: both off (LLDP effectively disabled on this port).

Security implication: LLDP's split enables receive-only monitoring — you learn about neighbors without revealing your own device info. CDP cannot do this; it is all-or-nothing per port. In security-sensitive deployments LLDP's granularity is a meaningful advantage.

Verification: show cdp interface simply lists ports that are enabled. show lldp interface shows Tx and Rx states on separate lines per port, reflecting the split.

Exam framing: 'Which L2 discovery protocol supports independent transmit and receive control?' → LLDP. 'Which command disables CDP on one port?' → no cdp enable. 'Which command disables only LLDP sending?' → no lldp transmit. Expect 1–2 questions on this difference.

hack.memory: CDP = 1 toggle per port (cdp enable). LLDP = 2 toggles per port (transmit, receive). LLDP wins in security flexibility. Exam: 'which protocol supports receive-only?' → LLDP, always.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: on a CDP-only adjacency, try to configure 'receive-only' — you can't. CDP has no such command. Switch to LLDP: no lldp transmit + lldp receive → receive-only works. This 60-second contrast cements the split-control difference.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: near-guaranteed comparison question. The split-control advantage is LLDP's marquee feature differentiator.

2.3.g.info:
CDP and LLDP are fully independent protocols — you can run them simultaneously on the same Cisco device, on the same ports, at the same time. They use different multicast MACs, different EtherTypes, different frame formats, and maintain separate neighbor tables. Neither interferes with the other.

Why this works technically:

  • CDP uses destination MAC 01:00:0C:CC:CC:CC and a SNAP-framed payload. Only Cisco devices recognize and process these frames.
  • LLDP uses destination MAC 01:80:C2:00:00:0E and EtherType 0x88CC. Any LLDP-capable device processes these.
  • The two frame types coexist on the same wire without conflict — they look like unrelated L2 multicast traffic to each other.

When to run both: In any mixed-vendor network. Cisco-to-Cisco adjacencies still benefit from CDP's Cisco-specific fields (VTP domain, native VLAN as distinct TLVs). Cisco-to-non-Cisco adjacencies require LLDP for visibility. Running both covers every case.

Configuration:

Switch(config)# cdp run        ! CDP is already on by default
Switch(config)# lldp run       ! enable LLDP additionally
!
! That's it. Both are now running on every interface.

Neighbor tables are separate: show cdp neighbors lists only CDP-discovered neighbors (Cisco). show lldp neighbors lists only LLDP-discovered neighbors (any vendor). A Cisco neighbor that runs both CDP and LLDP will appear in BOTH tables on the local switch.

CPU/bandwidth impact: Minimal. A typical switch sees a few hundred bytes per minute per port for CDP and a few hundred more for LLDP. The combined overhead is rounding-error on any modern Ethernet link.

Industry best practice: Enable both. Disable per-interface (no cdp enable, no lldp transmit) only on untrusted ports where the information leakage risk matters.

Exam framing: 'Can CDP and LLDP both run on the same device?' → Yes. 'Do they interfere?' → No. This is an easy 1-point question that rewards reading carefully.

hack.memory: CDP + LLDP = yes, together, no conflict. Different MACs, different EtherTypes, separate tables. Enable both in mixed-vendor environments. Cisco-Cisco links fill both tables; mixed links fill only LLDP.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer with 3 devices: Cisco switch, Cisco router, generic switch. Run both cdp run (default on) and lldp run. show cdp neighbors — only the Cisco router. show lldp neighbors — Cisco router AND generic switch. The Cisco router appears twice because it runs both protocols.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34 ends with the 'both together' scenario. OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 1 easy question. 'Can both be enabled at once?' → Yes — a common distractor is 'No, they conflict,' which is wrong.
